under the conflict-of-interest

Reform
Act (the rras;rr 1 . 1
OUESTTONS
May Directors Mason, Pierini, and onysko participate j-n

Tahoe Redevelopment Agency?
CONCLUSIONS
Directors tttason, Pierini, and onysko rnay participate in the

Tahoe Redevelopment Agency, if the decisions will not have a
reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generaLLy, on
themselves or their economic interests.

FACTS
The South Lake Tahoe Public Utility 
District (the "distri.ct")
provides sewer service for collection, treatment, and dj.sposal of
sewage to approximately 17,000 connections, and water service for
approximateLy L2, ooo connections in the city of souttr Lake Tahoe
(the ttcity'r) . The city is included within the boundaries of the
district 
but is snaller in size than the district. 
The city
formed the South Lake Tahoe Redevelopment Aqency (the rragencyrr),
which has adopted a redevelopment plan for development of a
portion of the city (the rtredevelopment area'r). This plan is
proposed to be irnplemented in two or more phases. The first 
phase
1s ieferred to as Redevelopment Project No. 1. The following
projects are included in Redevelopment Project No. 1:
Tahoe Marina Hote1 (298 hotel rooms)
Embassy suites (4oo hotel suite units)
Some affordable housing
Linear parklray
wet lands project
Roadway and drainage inprovements (Loop Road)
Redevelopment Project No. 1 will require 1,813 sewer units,
equivalent to 568-plus single fanily residential unit sewer
requirements, and water service. A seirer unit j.s a measurement of
estimated ffow and strenqth of sewage connected to district
facilities.
older notels are being acquired and destroyed by
Redevelopnent Project No. l-. These motels, which have been or
will be destroyed, utilize L,813 sewer units, the nurober needed
for Redevelopment Project No. 1.
The district 
is required to furnish sewer and water service
to Redevelopurent Project No. 1 if facilities 
and capacity are
available or can be nade available. 
The district 
is required by
law to charge the estinated cost of service. The district 
has a
transfer-of-sewer-unit ordinance providing for the transfer of
sewer units from a parcel- upon which the developrnent is destroyed
to a new undeveloped or previously-developed parcel requiring
sewer units for service. There are certain conditions of transfer
required under the ordinance which some of the projects in
Redevelopment Project No. L may not be able to neet. The transfer
ordj-nance provides that the board of directors of the district 
may
provide relief from these conditions in its discretion.
If the transfer-of-sewer-unit ordinance i.s not aPplicable to
all of the sewer services required by the RedeveloPnent Project
No. 1 and relief is not authorized by the district 
board of
directors, some of the projects in Redevelopment Project No. 1
wilf be required to obtain a nevJ connection for sewer services.

The district 
has established a capacity or connection charqe for
new sewer connections under a separate ordinance.
The district 
has no transfer of water connecti-on ordinance.
AI1 r{ater connections for development are treated as new
connections.
The paynent of the estirnated costs for Redevelopment Project
No. 1 by the agency to the district 
for sewer and water service
can be accomplished through a memorandum of understanding executed
by the directors of the district 
and the agency. The payment of
the estinated costs by the agency can also be accomplished by the
district 
board of directors grantinqr relief fron an existing
district 
ordinance and amending an existing district 
ordinance.
Either procedure requires action by the board of directors to
charge the estinated cost and provide sewer and water service to
Redevelopment Project No. 1.
DIRECTOR MASON
Director Mason provj-des architectural services to numerous
clients in the city of south Lake Tahoe. Ile has received rnore
than $250 in the previous 12 months from the city of South Lake
Tahoe for architectural services performed for the city regarding
a shop. This shop is located outside, and over 2,500 ft. from the
boundaries of, the redevelopment area.
Director Mason has perforned architectural- services for the
Lake Tahoe Inn, a motel of approximately 400 units, which is
located within the redevelopment area near E'nbassy Suj-tes, the
proposed hotel which is part of Redevelopment Project No. 1. 
He
has received more than $250 in the previous 12 months from Lake
Tahoe Inn. Director Mason has also Perforned architectural
services for Brooks Lodger which is located within the
redevelopnent area. These services are expected to be completed
during the month of March l-990 and Director Mason expects to
receive over S25o following completion of that project.
Director Mason has performed architectural services for Inn
by the Lake. This project involved adding a garalre and three
tennis courts on top of the garage. The fnn by the Lake is
located outside, and over 2,500 ft- from the boundaries of, the
redevelopment area. Director Mason has also done some resj-dential
designs for projects which are located outside, and over 2,5oo ft.
from the boundaries of, the redevelopment area.
Director Mason owns the office building in which his
architectural practice is located. This building is located
outside, and more than 2,500 ft. fron the boundaries of, the
redevelopment area' Director Mason also ovrns a residence which is
Located 6utside, and more than 2,5o0 ft. from the boundaries of,
the redevelopnent area.

DIRECTOR PIERINI
Director Pierini is an equal partner in the Lake Tahoe coin
and Bullion business (the rrcoin businessrr). Prior to July 14,
1989, the coin business leased prenises from Fantasy Inn
Partnership. These premises were part of a motel and business
property vrhich was one of the older motels being acquired and
destroyed for Redevelopment Project No. l-. subsequent to Ju]'y 14,
1989, Lhe coin business was relocated to a point outside, and more
than 2,500 ft. from the boundaries of, the redevelopment area-
Director Pierini and his partner are currently engaged in seekingr
compensatj.on for the terminated leasehold interest from the agency
and Fantasy Inn Partnership in an eminent domain proceeding.
Director pierini and his partner have cross-complained for tort
damages against rantasy Inn Partnership, the aqJency and the.city
in tie eninent doroain proceeding as a result of the manner in
which they were evicted. The lega1 action is still 
pending.
Director Pieri-ni owns a home and a vacant lot which are
located outside, and over 2,500 ft. from the boundaries of, the
redevelopment area.
DIRECTOR ONYSKO
Director onysko is a professional engineer and is emPloyed on
a sal-ary basis by Jere E. wittians and Associates, an engineerinqr
firm in nound tti1I, Nevada. Director onysko is not entitled to

Jere E. Williaurs and Associates is currently under contract
with the city of south Lake Tahoe for engj.neering work on the Loop
Road Project. 
The contract is a joint venture q'ith other
engineering, environmental and architectural f j-rrns. The contract

The Loop Road Project wil] reguj're the reLocation of
utilities, 
including water and sewer Lines' If the water and
sewer lines located-in the city streets interfere with the Loop
Road project they $roufd have to be rel,ocated at district 
expense.
lhe prop6sed menorandum of understanding provides that the agency
,iff'p"y 
the cost of utility 
line relocation for the Loop Road
Proj ect .
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public officiat 
fron making,
participating in, oi using his official 
position to influence a
i""ai".'""t"1-decision 
in wnicn he knows or has reason to know he

interest.

on the public ggnerally, on the official 
or a menber of his irn-
mediate family, z or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment Lrorth
one thousand dol,lars ($1,00o) or more.
(b) Any reaf property in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a coltrmercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,

within 12 months prior to the time when the

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
enployee, or holds any position of management '
Section 87103 (a) to (d) .
The members of the board of directors of the district 
are
public official-s. 
(Section 82048.) Accordingly, they may.not
ilake, participate in making, or attemPt to use their of f ici'al-
positi-on to i-nfl.uence a grovernmental decj.sion if the decision wilL
irarr" a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect,
distingrrishable from the effect on the Dublic ognerall-v, on
thernseives or their economic interests as described by section
87103.
Foreseeabi l itv
The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that they will occur. To be foresee-
able, the effects of a decision must be more than a nere possibil-
ity; however certainty is not reguired. 
(Downev cares v' Downev
Coimunitv Development Com. (1987) 196 cal. APP' 3d 983 | 989-99L;
Witt r/. Morrol/ (lg77l 70 Cal. App. 3d 817, 822; In re Thprner
(1r?5) 1 rPPC Ops. 198 (copy enclosed). ) The Act seeks to prevent
.o." th.t actual conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even
the appearance of a possible conflict of j-nterest' 
(Witt v' Mor-
ro9r, supra at 823. )
2  An Officia■′s ':immediate fami■y:! inc■udes his spouse and
dependent chi■dren。  (SeCtiOn 82029。
)

Materia l ity
RegRrlation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
whether an official's 
economic interest in a decision is rrmateri-
allytt affected as required by section 871o3. If the offi.cial's
economic interest is directlv invol,ved in the decision, then
Regulation LA7o2.1 (copy enclosed) applies to determine material-
ity. 
on the other hand, if the official's 
econonic interest is
indirectlv affected by the decision, then Regulations 18702.2 to
La7o2.6 (copies encLosed) would apply to deterurine r.rhether the
effect of the decision is rnaterial.
Public cenerallv
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is naterial, disqualification is reguired only if the
effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public generall'y.
(Section 87103.) For the district, 
the public consj.sts of .aII
residents of the district. 
Thus, disqualification is required
unless the decision $rill affect the directors' econonic interests
in substantially the same manner as it will affect all residents
of the district, 
or a significant segrnent of the residents of the
aistiict. 
lnegnrtation 1,6'703, copy enclosed. ).3
DIRE TOR MASON
The Act requires that Director Mason disqualify hinself frorn
participating in decisions which will have a reasonably
ioreseeable and material- financial effect, distingmishable from
the effect on the putrlic generally, on his economic interests,
such as the sources of income to him, his rea]- estate interests,
and his investment interests.
(a) The citv: 
The city is a source of incorne to Director
Mason of more than $250 in the 12 months precedingr the decision.
Director ltason must, therefore, disqualify himself from
participating in decisions which will have a material financial
effect on the city which is distingruishable from the effect on the
public Aenerally. This disqualification is required unless the
decision will affect the Director Mason's econonic interests in
substantially the sarBe manner as it wiLl affect all residents of
the district, 
or a sionificant secrment of the residents of the
district. 
(Regnrlation 18703. )

As noted above, for the district the public consists of the
residents of the district. 
In order to be considered a
significant segTnent of the public, a group nust be large in
numbers and heterogeneous in quality. 
(In re Ferraro (L978) 4
FPPC Ops. 62; In re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1.) The residents of
the city constitute such a group and, therefore, are deemed to be
a significant segment of the public irithin the meaning of
Regulation l-8703.' Therefore, any financial effect on the city
will not be distingpishable from the effect on the Public
generally. Accordingly, Dj-rector llason's receipt of income from
the city in the 12 months preceding the decision does not require
him to disqualify hinself fron participating in the decisions
regarding sewer and $rater service to the agency.
(b) Lake Tahoe Inn and Brooks Lodqe: Director Mason has
performed services for Lake Tahoe Inn and Brooks Lodge and has
ieceived or expects to receive more than $z5o froro each of thern in
the 12 months prior to the decj.sions. Thus, Lake Tahoe Inn and
Brooks Lodge are sources of income to Director Mason within the
meaninq of Section 87L03(c). Therefore, he may not participate in
the deaisions regarding serrer and lrater service to the agency if
the decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable and material
financial effect, distinguishable fron the effect on the public
generally, on either of the sources of incone.
As discussed above, to be foreseeable, the effects of a
decision must be nore than a nere possibility; 
however, certainty
is not required. In response to my questions, in your letter
dated Apri,1 23, l99O you clarified that, under one of the
scenarios discussed, it was reasonably foreseeable that the
decisions would have a financial effect on Lake Tahoe Inn and
Brooks Lodge.
Depending on the size of the business entity, one of the
subdivilions of negnrlation 18702.2 woul-d apply to deterrnine
whether the financial effect of the decisions on Lake Tahoe Inn
and Brooks Lodge is rnateriaf . such financial effect on Lake Tahoe
Inn or Brooks Lodge woul'd be distinguishable from the effect on
the public qeneraiLy. Accordingly, if the effect of the decisions
r"g.iding s6wer and-water serviie to the agency is deemed material,
puisuant to Regulation 1a702.2, Director Mason nust disqualify
hirosetf from participating in the decisions.
(c) Inn bv the Lake: - Director Mason has performed services
for Inn' Uy-EttE-Iatc" ana fras received, presurnablY, ngrg than $25o
for such iervices in the 12 months prior to the decisions. Under
such circumstances, Director Mason must disqualify himseLf if the

sections.question:
S
May Directors Mason, Pierini, and onysko participate j-n
decisions regarding sewer and water service to the South Lake
Tahoe Redevelopment Agency?
sections.conclusion:
Directors tttason, Pierini, and onysko rnay participate in the
decj.sions regarding sewer and water service to the south Lake
Tahoe Redevelopment Agency, if the decisions will not have a
reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generaLLy, on
themselves or their economic interests.

The South Lake Tahoe Public Utility 
District (the "distri.ct")
provides sewer service for collection, treatment, and dj.sposal of
sewage to approximately 17,000 connections, and water service for
approximateLy L2, ooo connections in the city of souttr Lake Tahoe
(the ttcity'r) . The city is included within the boundaries of the
district

QUESTION: S
May Directors Mason, Pierini, and onysko participate j-n
decisions regarding sewer and water service to the South Lake
Tahoe Redevelopment Agency?

CONCLUSION: Directors tttason, Pierini, and onysko rnay participate in the
decj.sions regarding sewer and water service to the south Lake
Tahoe Redevelopment Agency, if the decisions will not have a
reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generaLLy, on
themselves or their economic interests.

Wiedman Attorney, south Tahoe Public Utility District 325 l,{ain street Placerville, cA 95667 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-9 0-211 Dear Mr. Wiedrnan: This is in response to your request for advice regarding the responsibilities of south Lake Tahoe Public utility District Directors Robert ltason, Luis J. Pierini, and Steven J. onysko under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the rras;rr 1 . 1 OUESTTONS May Directors Mason, Pierini, and onysko participate j-n decisions regarding sewer and water service to the South Lake Tahoe Redevelopment Agency? CONCLUSIONS Directors tttason, Pierini, and onysko rnay participate in the decj.sions regarding sewer and water service to the south Lake Tahoe Redevelopment Agency, if the decisions will not have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generaLLy, on themselves or their economic interests. 1^ covernment code Sections 81000-91015. AL1 statutory references are to the Government code unless otherwise indicated. Comrni-ssion regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations. section 18ooo, et se(I. AIl references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 5 of the California code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 . (916) 3 22'5660 Our File No. A-9 0-211 Page 2 FACTS The South Lake Tahoe Public Utility District (the "distri.ct") provides sewer service for collection, treatment, and dj.sposal of sewage to approximately 17,000 connections, and water service for approximateLy L2, ooo connections in the city of souttr Lake Tahoe (the ttcity'r) . The city is included within the boundaries of the district but is snaller in size than the district. The city formed the South Lake Tahoe Redevelopment Aqency (the rragencyrr), which has adopted a redevelopment plan for development of a portion of the city (the rtredevelopment area'r). This plan is proposed to be irnplemented in two or more phases. The first phase 1s ieferred to as Redevelopment Project No. 1. The following projects are included in Redevelopment Project No. 1: Tahoe Marina Hote1 (298 hotel rooms) Embassy suites (4oo hotel suite units) Some affordable housing Linear parklray wet lands project Roadway and drainage inprovements (Loop Road) Redevelopment Project No. 1 will require 1,813 sewer units, equivalent to 568-plus single fanily residential unit sewer requirements, and water service.
